meta.enrichment_note: Deterministic verdicts preserved verbatim. Bare planner abstains resolved by reasoned review; recomputable between-group p-values were reproduced with a stdlib two-sided t-test (regularized incomplete beta) before classifying. field=medicine / RCT statistics. Integrity (T7) screen of the n=20 tables surfaced no Benford/terminal-digit/duplication signal.

findings.message: reported total equals the sum of its parts (within printing precision)

routed_to_human.note: between-group Welch t-test on Test1 (168±47,n=8 vs 213±72,n=12) reproduces p=0.108 (recomputed 0.1081); no over-reach.
routed_to_human.quote:
did not reach
statistical significance ( p = 0.108)

routed_to_human.note: mean difference recomputes exactly (168-213=-45) and the CI crosses zero, coherent with the non-significant p=0.108; no over-reach on the point estimate. (CI magnitude itself flagged separately as an advisory.)
routed_to_human.quote:
The supervised group
completed Test 1 in a mean of 168 s (SD 47), compared with
213 s (SD 72) in the independent group. The mean difference
was −45 s, with a 95% CI ranging from −108 to +18 s

routed_to_human.note: between-group Welch t-tests reproduce Test1 p=0.108 (0.1081) and Test2 p=0.124 (0.1260); 'slightly shorter' is faithful to the means; no over-reach.
routed_to_human.quote:
slightly shorter
in the supervised group compared with the independent group
( p = 0.108 and p = 0.124, respectively)

routed_to_human.note: between-group Welch t-test on Test3 gaming (137±22,n=11 vs 249±155,n=9) reproduces p=0.063 (recomputed 0.0627); reported as a non-significant trend; no over-reach.
routed_to_human.quote:
completed examination test 3 in a mean of
137 s, compared to 249 s for those without such experience
(p = 0.063)

routed_to_human.note: between-group Welch t-test on Test3 mobile (142±24,n=7 vs 212±139,n=13) reproduces p=0.102 (recomputed 0.1001); 'trend' is faithful; the prose/table mismatch (0.102 vs 0.101) is presentational.
routed_to_human.quote:
a trend toward faster
completion in test 3 (p = 0.102)

routed_to_human.note: between-group Welch t-test on Test3 by sex (men 140±33,n=6 vs women 208±134,n=14) reproduces p=0.092 (recomputed 0.0944); 'trend, slightly faster' is faithful; no over-reach.
routed_to_human.quote:
a trend for men to complete live test 3 slightly faster
(140 ± 33 s vs 208 ± 134 s; p = 0.092)

routed_to_human.note: 14/20 = 70.0% recomputes exactly; 'approximately 70%' is faithful; no over-reach.
routed_to_human.quote:
Approximately 70% of participants were
female

routed_to_human.note: between-group Welch t-test on baseline warm-up (388±109,n=8 vs 347±172,n=12) reproduces p=0.529 (recomputed 0.522); 'no significant baseline difference' is faithful; no over-reach.
routed_to_human.quote:
there was no significant difference in task completion 
time between the supervision and independent groups

routed_to_human.note: paired warm-up→Test1 improvement in the supervised arm (388→168, p<0.001); claim faithfully restates Table 2 and the magnitude is large and directionally unambiguous. Reviewed, no over-reach; no numeric verifier covers the paired p (within-subject SD/correlation not extracted) yet.
routed_to_human.quote: the Supervised group (388s vs 168s; p < 0.001)

routed_to_human.note: paired warm-up→Test1 improvement in the independent arm (347→213, p=0.020); claim faithfully restates Table 2. Reviewed, no over-reach; no numeric verifier covers the paired p (within-subject SD/correlation not extracted) yet.
routed_to_human.quote: the independent group (347s vs 213s; p = 0.020)

routed_to_human.note: within-group pre/post perceived-difficulty drop in the supervised arm (4.1→3.5, p=0.049); claim faithfully restates Table 3 and scopes it correctly ('after vs pre-training'). Reviewed, no over-reach; no numeric verifier covers the paired p yet.
routed_to_human.quote:
reported significantly lower perceived difficulty after training
compared to pre-training (4.1 vs 3.5; p = 0.049)

routed_to_human.note: within-group pre/post feedback-value drop in the independent arm (4.6→2.6, p=0.003); 'reduced to half' is approximate (half of 4.6 is 2.3 vs reported 2.6) but hedged and directionally faithful to Table 3. Reviewed, no over-reach; no numeric verifier covers the paired p yet.
routed_to_human.quote:
post-training score was significantly reduced to half of the
baseline value (4.6 vs 2.6; p = 0.003)

routed_to_human.note: within-group feedback value unchanged in the supervised arm (4.5→4.6, diff +0.1, p=0.685); claim faithfully restates Table 3 (the 0.684/0.685 prose/table rounding is noted in meta). Reviewed, no over-reach; no numeric verifier covers the paired p yet.
routed_to_human.quote:
remaining at the
same level (4.5 vs 4.6; p = 0.685)

routed_to_human.note: paired warm-up→Test1 by sex (women improved 173s p=0.001; men 159s p=0.058); claim faithfully restates the prose and correctly contrasts a significant vs a borderline result. Reviewed, no over-reach; no numeric verifier covers the paired p yet.
routed_to_human.quote:
women
improved their times significantly (173 s; p = 0.001) whereas men
showed a less pronounced improvement (159 s; p = 0.058)

routed_to_human.note: restatement of the prior-study effect used to power this trial (guided 2.1, control 0.6, SD 1.8 mm/s); an external (T1) fact matching the cited source's quoted values, not an internal recompute. Reviewed, no over-reach; no numeric verifier covers it yet.
routed_to_human.quote:
improved rope test 
performance by 2.1 mm/s, compared with a control group that 
improved by 0.6 mm/s (4). The standard deviation of the first 
group was 1.8 mm/s

routed_to_human.note: qualitative task description (Rope Race = thread through 8 loops); not a numeric claim. Reviewed, no over-reach; no numeric verifier covers it yet.
routed_to_human.quote:
pass a 
thread through eight loops arranged in a circle

routed_to_human.note: The reported p=0.037 for shooter vs non-shooter Test3 (130±13,n=5 vs 207±103,n=15) does not reproduce cleanly: a Welch t-test on those values gives p≈0.013 and a pooled t-test p≈0.119, neither close to 0.037, and the reported SD is internally inconsistent (prose gives non-shooter SD=130, Table 4 gives 103). With n=5 in one arm, normality/variance assumptions are fragile and the test used is unstated. What would confirm: the raw per-participant Test3 times, the exact test, and which SD is correct.
routed_to_human.quote:
completed test 3 significantly faster than those who did not
(130 ± 13 s vs 207 ± 130 s; p = 0.037)

routed_to_human.note: The reported 95% CI for the Test1 mean difference (−45 s; −108 to +18 s) does not reproduce from 168±47 (n=8) vs 213±72 (n=12): a pooled-variance CI is [−106, +16] and a Welch CI is [−101, +11]. The point estimate (−45) is exact and the interval correctly crosses zero (coherent with p=0.108), so the conclusion stands, but the interval is ~10–15% wider than either standard recompute. What would confirm: the exact SE/df and CI method used.
routed_to_human.quote:
The supervised group
completed Test 1 in a mean of 168 s (SD 47), compared with
213 s (SD 72) in the independent group. The mean difference
was −45 s, with a 95% CI ranging from −108 to +18 s

routed_to_human.note: The a-priori power analysis targets only 70% power (36 trainees), below the conventional 80%, and the study then enrolled 20 — about 55% of even that reduced target. The design is underpowered by the authors' own calculation before any data were collected, which bears on every null ('no significant difference') result. What would confirm: the effect size, alpha, and allocation ratio fed into the power calculation.
routed_to_human.quote:
a total of 
36 trainees would be required to achieve 70% power to detect a 
statistically significant difference between the groups

routed_to_human.note: Post-hoc 'achieved power' of 28–35% computed from the observed effect sizes is a known statistical fallacy: observed (retrospective) power is a deterministic monotone transform of the p-value and adds nothing beyond it (Hoenig & Heisey 2001). The honest framing is the wide confidence intervals, which the paper does report. What would confirm: nothing recomputable — this is a method-appropriateness concern, not an arithmetic one.
routed_to_human.quote:
the achieved power was 
approximately 28%–35%

routed_to_human.note: The abstract's causal conclusion over-reaches on two counts. (1) 'Significantly reduces perceived difficulty' rests on a within-arm pre/post change in the supervised group (4.1→3.5, p=0.049), not a supervised-vs-independent contrast, so it cannot support a comparative causal claim relative to independent practice. (2) 'Prevents the devaluation of feedback' is inferred from two separate within-arm tests (supervised stable p=0.685; independent dropped p=0.003) with no between-group or group×time interaction test reported. Both rest on n=20 at ~28–35% power. The causal/comparative verbs ('reduces','prevents') are stronger than a single underpowered RCT with un-interacted subgroup tests supports. What would confirm: a between-group (or group×time interaction) test on each questionnaire item.
routed_to_human.quote:
Remote real-time supervision significantly reduces perceived 
difficulty and prevents the devaluation of feedback compared to independent 
practice.

routed_to_human.note: Whether 'remote real-time supervision matters' for undergraduate laparoscopic training is the paper's headline take-home; its importance/worth is a value judgement (T8) that LITMUS surfaces but does not score.
routed_to_human.quote:
Remote real-time supervision significantly reduces perceived 
difficulty and prevents the devaluation of feedback compared to independent 
practice.

abstained.message: no numeric verifier covers this 'no significant difference' triple (per-sex SDs for Warm-up/Test1/Test2 not extracted); abstaining rather than absolving (DESIGN §3.4)

evidence.quote:

differences were found in Warm-up (p = 0.627), Test 1 (p = 0.639),
or Test 2 (p = 0.233)
